attributes.stem:
A 21-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with acute onset unsteadiness and visual disturbance. She has been unwell with an upper respiratory tract infection for the past seven days. She reports no difficulty with speech or swallowing. She is a non-smoker and drinks 3-4 standard drinks on the weekends.

On examination, she is afebrile, haemodynamically stable and oriented to time, person and place. On cranial nerve examination, pupils are equal and reactive to light, with no relative afferent pupillary defect. There is bilateral ptosis and marked limitation of eye adduction bilaterally, and she reports double vision on eye movements. There is no nystagmus. Facial movement and sensation is normal. Power is preserved in her upper and lower limbs, however deep tendon reflexes are absent bilaterally in the lower limbs. Sensation is reduced in the peripheries on both upper and lower limbs. Examination of gait reveals ataxia, without clear lateralisation, and difficulty walking heel-to-toe.

Her blood tests revealed elevated CRP but were otherwise unremarkable. Her MRI brain demonstrates no diffusion restriction. A lumbar puncture demonstrates 4 monocytes [0-4] and an elevated protein of 1.90g/L [0.2-.0.4]. The gram stain is negative and the culture is pending.
attributes.stemQuestion: Given the most likely diagnosis, which one of the following is the most appropriate management for this condition?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has ataxia, ophthalmoplegia, and areflexia, a triad consistent with Miller Fisher Syndrome (MFS), a variant of Guillain Barre syndrome (GBS). Just like GBS, it commonly presents 1-4 weeks after a viral upper respiratory tract infection. It is assumed to be caused by antibodies against a ganglioside (GQ1b). This ganglioside is commonly found in high levels in cranial nerves III, IV, and VI, which leads to the classic ophthalmoplegia. (Note: Paralysis of the lower extremities may be present but is less common as compared to GBS). CSF analysis classically reveals albuminocytologic dissociation (ie, elevated proteins without elevated white cells). Management of GBS and MFS involves IV immunoglobulin (IVIg) and/or plasmapheresis. Corticosteroids should be avoided in GBS and MFS.

IV corticosteroids are the first line treatment for acute exacerbation of multiple sclerosis. MS may present with symptoms similar to MFS, however, an MRI brain would reveal periventricular lesions, and CSF analysis may yield lymphocytic pleocytosis and oligoclonal bands.

IV chemotherapy may be considered for CNS lymphoma. Lymphoma affecting the cerebellum may present with ataxia but is unlikely to be associated with areflexia and albuminocytologic dissociation on CSF. Moreover, an MRI brain would typically reveal a mass lesion or leptomeningeal enhancement.

IV tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) would be appropriate in the case of a suspected acute ischaemic stroke. A brainstem stroke can present with hemiparesis, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia, nystagmus, or dysarthria depending on the location of the brainstem involved. An acute ischaemic stroke is diagnosed with an MRI brain.

IV thiamine is the first line treatment in patients suspected of Wernicke's encephalopathy, a condition caused by severe thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency, often due to chronic alcohol use. The alcohol history of this patient is not consistent with this diagnosis.
learningPoints: The triad of signs for Miller Fisher Syndrome are: ophthalmoplegia, ataxia and areflexia.
Miller Fisher Syndrome is associated with G1Qb antibodies
references: Berlit P, Rakicky J. The Miller Fisher syndrome. Review of the literature. J Clin Neuroophthalmol. 1992 Mar;12(1):57-63. PMID: 1532603.
Teener J. Miller Fisher's Syndrome. Seminars in Neurology. 2013;32(05):512-516.
